reason: PROP-029 safety net fired: 137 assigned-analyst ISSs have no expansion-tracker entry. Distribution is concentrated in 4 decider runs (2026-05-10-17-21 / 2026-05-11T05-01 / 2026-05-11T09-20 / 2026-05-11T13-20Z, ~30 orphans each = 120 orphans total in 48h window) plus a handful of older runs and 2 from a 2026-05-12 manual operator reroute. The 4-decider-run pattern is the dominant signal — likely a structural decoupling in those runs (decider routed ISSs to assigned-analyst without writing the matching tracker entry). The clustering at exactly 30 per run is suspicious — could indicate a batch-size cap. PROP-029 (CLAUDE.md says landed 2026-05-11) should have prevented orphans from creating in those very runs.
action_recommended: Tinker investigates: (1) why did decider runs 2026-05-10-17-21 through 2026-05-11T13-20Z create ~30 orphan ISSs each despite PROP-029 supposedly landing 2026-05-11? Possible causes: PROP-029 code path missed for the M1 mass-routing batch (vs single-ISS routing in steady-state), or PROP-029 only covers the ROUTE-TO-ANALYST path and not a parallel batch-routing path. (2) Read tinker proposal queue for an existing item; add a sibling proposal for the closure-decoupling pattern observed in EXP-366 (decider closes ISSs by c-N verification without popping tracker — 11 stale-tracker entries discovered this run). (3) Consider a one-shot remediation pass that mass-pops tracker entries for ISSs that are already closed (the closure-decoupling pattern) and mass-creates tracker entries for the 137 orphans (the routing-decoupling pattern).
action_taken_this_run: No mass-remediation attempted; EXP-366 (this run) drained 12 stale-tracker entries via closure-only verifications (11 ISSs already-closed + 1 patched). Net tracker pending: 27→15. Orphan count not touched (would require creating 137 tracker entries — out of scope for an analyst run; this is structural plumbing work for tinker/operator). The 4-decider-run cluster (Sun-Mon 2026-05-10/11) needs a dedicated remediation pass.

reason: Chapman Sq normalization applied (3 fields: detail_evidence, detail_verdict_text, tldr_verdict) and Kim & Chang DOI link added. Verify scientific framing unchanged.
SC pattern (2) inline gloss added to detail_evidence; double period removed from detail_extra. Verify gloss is scientifically accurate for the argument context.

action_recommended: tinker investigates: PROP-029 expects decider's M1 Priority 5b to write a tracker entry every time it sets ISS.status='assigned-analyst'. These two ISSs were routed via operator manual reroute (operator-manual-rereoute-2026-05-12T05-30) on 2026-05-12 with class_hint set on the ISS but without a matching expansion-tracker entry. Same shape as the d44e2670 (2026-05-10) instance that PROP-029 was authored to address. Recommendation: (a) decider should add an orphan-scan step at start of every M1 that detects status='assigned-analyst' with no tracker entry and self-writes one (mirror of analyst's own orphan check); OR (b) any operator-manual-reroute procedure documented in tinker.md should be updated to require dual-write (set status + write tracker entry); OR (c) accept that operator manual reroutes are a low-frequency exception and rely on analyst's PROP-029 safety net to rescue (which is what happened here).
action_taken_this_run: Both orphan ISSs noted. EXP-383 authored for ISS-1921 (the holistic-tagged WIN-034 verdict reclassification) — tracker entry created by analyst-Opus during EXP authoring. ISS-1957 (verification-class-hint, narrow ΔB station-specificity patch on WIN-014) intentionally LEFT as orphan for this run — per PROP-034 baby-ownership boundary rule, ISS-1957's class_hint='verification' makes it baby-owned territory even though severity='major' qualifies it for the Opus safety-net filter; the Opus-owned-orphan filter is overinclusive in this edge case. Recommendation: baby's next 2h cycle should pick up ISS-1957 directly via its own orphan-pickup mechanism (severity='major' + class_hint='verification' is a legitimate baby case for verification-class EXP authoring under PROP-034). If baby's filter doesn't catch it (because PROP-034 hands major-severity to Opus), a separate hand-off note should be authored.

reason: Decider applied narrow patch (c8 H1): dropped "and any close perturbation thereof" from Bessel eigenvalue parenthetical in detail_evidence. EXP-377 analysis referenced this parenthetical — verify the dropped phrase does not create coherence gaps.

Analyst-opus run 2026-05-16T03:58Z encountered ENOSPC during EXP-377-pickup-ISS-1750 authoring. Disk df -h / showed 595M free, 9.0G/9.6G used (94%). Bash node fs.writeFileSync to /sessions/jolly-dreamy-planck/dome-review-clean/monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-377-pickup-ISS-1750.json failed with errno -28 ENOSPC. Investigation: /tmp/kev-repo (126M) is owned by another agent and cannot be removed (Permission denied across all files - likely owned by KEV-analysis pipeline running concurrently in same sandbox). dome-review-clean takes 541M, kev-repo 126M, /var/lib/apt 294M, /var/log/journal 1020M (cleanup attempts failed - rm and journalctl/apt clean did not free space). Workaround: wrote EXP file via FUSE-direct file tool (workspace path), which uses different filesystem and succeeded. Tracker entry updated via FUSE-direct python in-place mutation. Workspace-sync universal-pusher will copy expansion + proposal to git on its next hourly cycle. Proposal-ISS-1750-resolution.json also written via FUSE-direct.

Root cause hypothesis: kev-analysis scheduled task and dome-analyst share the same sandbox host disk; concurrent runs accumulate clones until disk fills. The CLAUDE.md per-session-clone-discipline rule covers operator-side cleanup but does not cover scheduled-agent cross-project sharing.

Action recommended: tinker investigates whether (a) the scheduler should isolate per-project disk quotas, (b) scheduled agents should check df before clone and abort/escalate if <1GB free, (c) /var/log/journal vacuum should run more aggressively in the agent sandbox image, or (d) cross-project /tmp directories should have a shared cleanup policy. The immediate workaround (FUSE-direct write + workspace-sync rescue) preserves the work but leaves git-side state lagging until next workspace-sync cycle.

PROP-029 safety net fired on Mode 1 dispatch (analyst run 2026-05-16T21:07:16.190Z): ISS-2026 (WIN-052 c5 horizon-analogy defense, major) was routed status='assigned-analyst' by decider-2026-05-16T09-19Z at 2026-05-16T09:22:14Z, but no matching expansion-tracker entry was written by the decider. Analyst created the tracker entry (EXP-406) while picking up the orphan, but the decider-side decoupling needs tinker root-cause investigation. Possible causes per analyst-mode1-expansions.md: (a) decider tracker-write failed transiently for this ISS, (b) a manual operator reroute set status without tracker-write, (c) a future agent variant introduced a code path that sets status without tracker-write. ISS-2026's routed_by_run is decider-2026-05-16T09-19Z — please check that decider run's logs for tracker-write attempt evidence and the run's M1 Priority 5b path execution. Note: the decider's routing_reason on ISS-2026 also misstates the EXP id ('EXP-395 created. (EXP id corrected from EXP-3771798)') — EXP-395 is actually the unrelated ISS-1130/1147/1164 EXP authored 2026-05-16T06:56Z. So the decider claimed it created an EXP for ISS-2026 but actually didn't. Both issues (no tracker write + incorrect EXP-id claim) point to the same M1 Priority 5b code path being broken or misrouted for this ISS.
action_recommended: tinker investigates: (1) what M1 Priority 5b code path the decider-2026-05-16T09-19Z run took for ISS-2026 — did the tracker-write fail silently? (2) where the 'EXP-395 created' claim in ISS-2026.routing_reason originated — that EXP id was already allocated for ISS-1130/1147/1164 (authored 06:56Z, three hours earlier). Either way, root-cause and patch the decoupling. Also: cross-check that no other ISSs from the same decider run are missing tracker entries.
action_taken_this_run: 1 orphan grouped into 1 EXP entry (EXP-407 (was EXP-406 in initial draft; renumbered mid-run due to FUSE collision), deep-attack class, ready-for-integration); decider issue-proposal proposal-ISS-2026-resolution.json authored to signal completion; tracker entry EXP-407 (was EXP-406 in initial draft; renumbered mid-run due to FUSE collision) added with source='orphan-pickup' and source_note flagging the decoupling for audit trail.

reason: EXP ID-allocation race continues. EXP-407 has TWO files in FUSE: (1) EXP-407-WIN-052-horizon-analogy-preemption.json (created 2026-05-16T21:06Z, tracker entry id=EXP-407 references this one); (2) EXP-407-SEC-4.2.7a-penumbra-scaling-correction.json (created later, applied by decider 2026-05-17T01:24Z to close ISS-1643). Decider used the penumbra one under "EXP-407" label without tracker reconciliation; the horizon-analogy EXP-407 tracker entry shows status=complete pointing at the other file. Two distinct EXPs sharing one ID. Previous analyst flagged this as third such collision in 24h (EXP-407, EXP-409 collisions). Recommended for tinker: investigate ID-allocation race causes (decider next_id=397 in tracker but actual max EXP file id=410 — tracker next_id is desynced from filesystem state).
recommended_action: Investigate EXP ID-allocation race: (a) tighten the prelude rebase to immediately before EXP authoring (not just at run-start); (b) add a server-side allocator/lock or atomic ID-reservation; (c) reconcile expansion-tracker.next_id with actual max EXP file ID seen across FUSE + clone; (d) document a renumber-and-stub protocol for the inevitable collisions until (b) is implemented. Third occurrence in 24h per prior analyst note 2026-05-17T01:14.

reason: Two Opus analyst runs ~1h apart (2026-05-17T02:13-02:15Z and 2026-05-17T03:11Z) performed 100% duplicate Mode 1 work on the same 12 baby-orphan-ISS-* deep-attack items. Both runs drafted EXP-411 cluster audit + 7 issue-proposals + 10 tracker flips covering ISS-1735/1736/1737/1738/1739/1740/1753 (and ISS-1743 in the first run). The second (03:11Z) run discovered the collision at FUSE-write time and discarded its clone-side drafts. Root cause: no cross-run claim mechanism on Mode 1 deep-attack items beyond the post-work tracker status flip. The Step 0a clean-clone refresh shrinks but does NOT eliminate the stale-clone window — the first run completed its tracker flips AFTER the second run's prelude clone but BEFORE the second run's FUSE-write step, so the second run saw a stale snapshot. Finding originally surfaced in analyst latest-analysis-summary.txt at git workspace-sync commit 15fbd4a (2026-05-17T04:06Z) but was overwritten by the subsequent 04:05Z analyst run's summary — recreated here as a durable inbox item per operator request 2026-05-17 ~08:50Z. Cost of the duplicate run: ~1 Opus analyst run wasted on 100%-replayed work; no scientific or pipeline-state damage (the no-op discard worked correctly).
source: analyst-opus run 2026-05-17T03:13:44Z (originally in OPERATOR / TINKER NOTE section of overwritten summary); inbox-recreated 2026-05-17T09:00Z by operator+cowork-claude
recommended_action:
Investigate analyst cross-run-claim mechanism for Mode 1 deep-attack items. Three candidate mitigations from the originating run's summary (analyst should pick one, recommend to operator):
(a) Longer minimum spacing between analyst runs when no new dispatcher-fire signal arrived since the prior run (e.g., suppress run if last_run_finished_at < 15 minutes ago AND no new tracker/inbox/hnote signals).
(b) Cross-run claim file in workspace-mounted state, checked at end of prelude (after clone refresh): "monitor/analyst/active-claim.json" written by run-N at workstart with run-id + claimed_items[], cleared at run-end; run-N+1's prelude reads it and skips claimed items.
(c) Per-tracker-entry claimed_by + claimed_at fields written at WORKSTART (not workend) in the clone AND immediately copied to FUSE so concurrent runs see a real claim; the current claimed_by is set post-work as part of the status flip.
Related: PROP-043 (analyst-operator-commission-priority, design-pending-operator-review) touches the same dispatcher infrastructure. PROP-043 does NOT address cross-run concurrency; the operator may want tinker to either (i) bundle this cross-run-claim fix into PROP-043, (ii) file as a sibling PROP, or (iii) defer until PROP-043 lands. Recommend tinker decides bundle-or-sibling based on whether the fix touches dispatcher.md or only the claim-file/tracker schema.
Related: this finding is itself an instance of the "analyst summary-write gap" pattern (DIRECTIVE-20260514-001 / PROP-036) — the original surfacing only persisted in the run summary which got overwritten. Worth noting in any follow-up PROP that "OPERATOR / TINKER NOTE" sections of analyst summaries should be auto-promoted to inbox items at write-time.
